claim: WHO reports 608,000 malaria deaths in 2022, 95 percent in Sub-Saharan Africa, with 76 percent among children under five, and identifies vector control, chemoprevention, and early diagnosis as core interventions.
method: Reviewed WHO fact sheet for global burden, transmission, control strategies, and climate context statements.
limitations: Global fact sheet, not a sub-national dataset.
Burden figures are modeled estimates, not direct counts.

claim: WHO World Malaria Report 2024 documents that climate change and extreme weather events are increasing malaria risk through changing transmission dynamics, with particular concern for seasonal forecasting reliability.
method: Reviewed WHO World Malaria Report executive summary and climate-relevant sections.
limitations: Report-level analysis, not district-level operational data.
Climate statements are directional, not quantitative thresholds.

claim: A 2025 scoping review of malaria early warning systems found that EWS often use climatic and environmental variables to forecast malaria risk, but face significant challenges in development and implementation including limited operational deployment evidence and evaluation gaps.

source.title: Advancing Early Warning Systems for Malaria: Progress, challenges, and future directions - A scoping review

method: Reviewed the PubMed abstract and metadata for this PLOS Public Health scoping review, which comprehensively assessed malaria EWS settings, methods, performance, actions, and evaluation across published primary research and programmatic reports.
limitations: Scoping review evidence still needs replication for specific country contexts.
Operational deployment evidence remains thin across the studies reviewed.
The review is a scoping review, not a systematic review with meta-analysis, so quantitative performance comparisons are limited.
Published in 2025; some included studies may have been superseded by more recent work.

claim: CHIRPS provides daily and monthly rainfall estimates at 0.05-degree resolution across Sub-Saharan Africa from 1981 to present, generated by merging satellite imagery with station data, and is publicly available for research use without license restriction.

source.title: The climate hazards infrared precipitation with stations — a new environmental record for monitoring extremes

method: Reviewed dataset description, resolution specifications, temporal coverage, station integration method, and data access conditions from the Scientific Data paper. Confirmed public availability via CHC website.
limitations: Station density varies significantly across Sub-Saharan Africa; some sub-regions rely almost entirely on satellite estimates with no ground validation.
CHIRPS does not distinguish rainfall intensity from duration — both affect mosquito vector breeding conditions differently.
0.05-degree resolution (~5km) may not capture catchment-scale rainfall heterogeneity in mountainous or highly variable terrain.
Does not capture evapotranspiration, soil moisture, or standing water — all relevant to malaria vector habitat.

claim: DHIS2 is the primary district-level health information system used across Sub-Saharan Africa for malaria case reporting, tested cases, and intervention coverage, but access to national DHIS2 instances requires a Memorandum of Understanding with national health authorities and is not publicly available.
method: Reviewed DHIS2 platform documentation for data types, geographic grain, country coverage, and access conditions. Confirmed national instance access requires country-level institutional agreements.
limitations: Access requires a Memorandum of Understanding with national health ministries or WHO country offices — not publicly available for research without institutional approval.
Data quality, completeness, and reporting lag vary significantly by country and district.
Passive surveillance data reflects health-seeking behavior and reporting capacity, not true disease incidence.
Administrative boundary versions in DHIS2 may differ from other administrative datasets, creating linkage challenges.
This evidence record classifies DHIS2 as a limited source for this pack: accessible in principle but requiring institutional negotiation not completed at this stage.
